entities.name: necromancer
entities.raw_quote: he threatened to go to a certain necromantic wizard [maleficum quendam nigromanticum] and find out through him about this strange affair.

entities.name: boy medium
entities.raw_quote: the necromancer... anointed the fingernail of a young boy, then with his incantations inquired of him what he saw.

entities.name: Berith
entities.raw_quote: within that circle they were to invoke the demon Berith, who would answer their inquiries.

entities.name: AGLA
entities.raw_quote: writes on it the letters ‘AGLA’... 'Lord Jesus Christ, king of glory, send us three angels to tell us the truth and not falsehood in all matters about which we shall inquire.'

entities.name: scrying
entities.raw_quote: The general term for such divination is ‘scrying’, which is sometimes subdivided into specific forms such as catoptromancy (divination by means of a mirror), crystallomancy (by a crystal), cyclicomancy or lecanomancy (by a cup or basin filled with liquid)...

entities.name: John Dee’s ‘show-stone’
entities.raw_quote: One particularly dramatic notion about the magical properties of mirrors is the legend... John Dee’s ‘show-stone’, through which Edward Kelly and other mediums communicated with angels.

entities.name: John Hartlieb’s procedures
entities.raw_quote: Johannes Hartlieb tells how a zaubermaister stands behind a child and recites secret words into his ear in the practice of hydromancy... he conjures the child and his nail with a long conjuration and recites three secret words into his ear.

entities.name: Maurice
entities.raw_quote: applied ointment to the thumbnail of a boy called Maurice, asked this medium what he saw there revealed...

entities.name: Brother Thomas Asty
entities.raw_quote: learned that the culprit was one of the canons, Brother Thomas Asty.

entities.name: William Lee
entities.raw_quote: they charged one William Lee, a poor man, who had...

summary: This chunk discusses medieval scrying-based divination, especially using children as mediums who gaze into polished surfaces (nails, mirrors, swords) to reveal hidden knowledge. It includes examples from judicial records and manuscripts such as the Rawlinson necromantic manuscript, referencing demon Berith and angelic invocations with the formula 'AGLA'. The text emphasizes the role of demons in providing visions and the theological concern over false revelations.
key_passages: within that circle they were to invoke the demon Berith, who would answer their inquiries.

The general term for such divination is ‘scrying’, which is sometimes subdivided into specific forms such as catoptromancy (divination by means of a mirror), crystallomancy (by a crystal), cyclicomancy or lecanomancy (by a cup or basin filled with liquid)...
he threatened to go to a certain necromantic wizard [maleficum quendam nigromanticum] and find out through him about this strange affair.
the demon who was providing information through the boy could no longer do so once the thief had been absolved.
